text: Hello everybody. This is a case of right sided ovarian cyst of a thirty three year young woman who completed her family.
She came with lower abdominal pain and heaviness for last three months.
The surgeon identifies and removes adhesions surrounding the ovarian cyst prior to performing the cystectomy.
The surgeon visually assesses the ovarian cyst, which measures approximately thirteen by twelve by eleven centimeters in diameter.
The surgeon examines the anatomical structures, noting that the right fallopian tube is adherent to the right-sided ovarian cyst, while the left-sided fallopian tube and ovary appear normal.
The surgeon performs the basic steps of laparoscopic cystectomy, focusing on the removal of the right-sided ovarian cyst while navigating the surgical field with precision.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to coagulate and dissect the cortex of a very tense ovarian cyst, visibly altering the cyst's surface upon contact.
The surgeon uses two non-tooth graspers to carefully dissect the cyst wall from the surrounding ovarian tissue, separating the structures with precision.
The surgeon carefully dissects the ovarian tissue from the cyst wall, maintaining a proper surgical plane to ensure precise separation of the structures.
We gently do this and we have done it as much as possible.
The cyst wall ruptures due to its uneven surface thickness during the laparoscopic cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates the cyst wall from the ovarian tissue using precise dissection techniques, maintaining clear anatomical visibility to prevent rupture.
The surgeon carefully dissects the cyst wall from the ovarian tissue using two non-tooth graspers, navigating a very thin layer that increases the risk of rupture.
The cyst has ruptured, and the surgeon inserts a sucker tube into the cyst to suction the cystic fluid.
The surgeon collects cystic fluid from outside the abdomen using a sterile syringe, preparing it for microscopic examination.
The surgeon uses a tooth holding grasper and a non-tooth grasper to carefully dissect the cyst wall from the ovarian tissue, aiming to separate these structures before further rupture occurs.
The surgeon carefully peels the cystic wall away from the ovarian tissue, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
If we have done it gently and a proper plane then we can avoid uneven blood loss.
The surgeon coagulates visible blood vessels on the remaining part of the cyst before cutting and separating the cystic wall from the ovarian tissue.
The surgeon irrigates the ovarian tissue with normal saline to identify any mild bleeding points, which are then coagulated to control bleeding.
The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery device to coagulate mild bleeding from the ovarian tissue, taking care to avoid excessive coagulation that could damage the tissue.
The surgeon places two or three stitches to secure the ovarian tissue after separating the cystic wall.
The surgeon uses suturing to achieve hemostasis in the ovarian cystectomy procedure, as the bipolar coagulation was insufficient for controlling bleeding.
On the other hand, suturing may be helpful to prevent adhesion formation in a gaped ovary after removal of a large ovarian tumor.
The surgeon removes excess suture material from the port side, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The surgeon carefully extracts the ovarian tissue from the surgical site, pulling it out through the port.
The surgeon performs repeated suction and irrigation within the surgical field, effectively removing all visible blood clots from the area.
